title: Neural Architecture Search via Differentiable Sampling: Bridging the Gap Between Supernet Training and Stand-Alone Performance
description: A 42-page NeurIPS-style paper proposing a new NAS method with extensive ablations but questionable novelty claims

findings.text: The core contribution (differentiable sampling via Gumbel-Softmax for operation selection) was previously proposed in GDAS (Searching for A Robust Neural Architecture in Four GPU Hours, Dong & Yang 2019). The paper cites GDAS but claims the difference is 'temperature annealing schedule' — this is an incremental modification, not a novel contribution.

Theorem 4.1 proves that the supernet gradient approximates the stand-alone gradient under 'mild conditions', but Condition C2 requires the operation weights to be nearly one-hot — which only holds at the end of search. The theorem does not provide guarantees during the critical early training phase.

The search space is limited to the DARTS cell-based space (8 candidate operations, 14 edges). No experiments on the larger NAS-Bench-301 or hardware-aware spaces demonstrate generality. The title claims 'bridging the gap' but the gap is only demonstrated in one specific search space.

Table 2 reports ImageNet top-1 accuracy of 76.8% with 580M FLOPs, but does not specify the training protocol (epochs, augmentation, regularization). EfficientNet-B0 achieves 77.1% at 390M FLOPs under standard training. Without controlled comparison of training recipes, architecture quality cannot be isolated.

The ablation study (Section 7) varies temperature schedule, search epochs, and architecture parameters simultaneously in Table 5. Proper ablation should vary one factor at a time with all others controlled to isolate individual contributions.

The paper measures 'rank correlation' between supernet and stand-alone accuracy (Table 3) using only 50 randomly sampled architectures. Given the search space size of ~10^18, this sample is insufficient. NAS-Bench-201 provides exact ground-truth for all architectures and should be used.

The limitations section acknowledges 'computational cost of the sampling procedure' but does not quantify it. Total GPU hours for search + evaluation should be compared with one-shot and training-free NAS baselines to demonstrate practical value.

Figure 3 shows the 'performance gap' closing over search epochs but uses a cherry-picked architecture example. Aggregated statistics (mean, median, quartiles) over multiple searched architectures would be more convincing.
